title: ignoreBuildErrors: true bypasses all TypeScript safety in CI/CD
description: typescript.ignoreBuildErrors is set to true in next.config.js. This means ANY TypeScript error (type mismatches, missing properties, undefined access, broken imports) will be silently ignored during build. The comment says 'Kept true to prevent CI failures from transient type generation issues' but this effectively disables the primary safety net for a strict-mode TypeScript project. Production code can ship with type errors that would cause runtime crashes.
fix: Set ignoreBuildErrors: false. If Prisma type generation causes transient CI issues, fix the root cause by ensuring 'prisma generate' runs reliably before the build step (which is already done in the CI pipeline). The comment acknowledges 0 errors as of 2026-03-16, so there is no reason to keep this enabled.

title: next-auth v5.0.0-beta.4 in production — unstable beta with known issues
description: The project uses next-auth@5.0.0-beta.4, which is an unstable beta release. Beta versions may have undiscovered security vulnerabilities, breaking changes, and missing features. The auth system is foundational to the entire application (admin, payments, MFA, OAuth). Using a beta for authentication in a production e-commerce platform handling payments is a significant risk.
fix: Upgrade to the latest stable next-auth v5 release when available, or pin to the latest beta that has been thoroughly tested. Monitor https://github.com/nextauthjs/next-auth/releases for stable releases. Consider adding a comment documenting why this specific beta version was chosen and what testing was done.

title: @types/nodemailer is in production dependencies instead of devDependencies
description: @types/nodemailer (line 42) is listed under 'dependencies' instead of 'devDependencies'. Type definition packages are only needed at build time for TypeScript compilation, not at runtime. Including them in production dependencies increases the deployment artifact size and install time.
fix: Move @types/nodemailer from 'dependencies' to 'devDependencies'.

title: playwright is a production dependency — adds ~100MB to deployment
description: The 'playwright' package (v1.58.2) is listed in 'dependencies' instead of 'devDependencies'. Playwright is a browser automation library used only for scraping (src/lib/scraper/google-maps-playwright.ts). Its Chromium browser binary adds ~100MB+ to the production build. On Azure App Service this wastes deployment time, storage, and container startup time.
fix: Move 'playwright' to devDependencies. If google-maps-playwright.ts needs it at runtime, refactor the scraper to use a separate service or make the import dynamic with a runtime check, or use a headless browser service API instead.

title: jssip listed in dependencies but never imported in src/
description: The jssip package (SIP.js alternative for VoIP) is listed in production dependencies but is never imported anywhere in src/. grep for 'from jssip' and 'require.*jssip' returns zero results. This is dead weight adding unnecessary bundle size.
fix: Remove jssip from dependencies. If it was replaced by @telnyx/webrtc (also not imported in src/), consider removing both unless they are used in client-side code loaded dynamically.

title: @telnyx/webrtc listed in dependencies but never imported in src/
description: The @telnyx/webrtc package is in production dependencies but no source file imports it. Search for 'from @telnyx/webrtc' returns zero results. This unused dependency increases install time and deployment size.
fix: Remove @telnyx/webrtc from dependencies if it's truly unused. If it's loaded via CDN or dynamic import in client code, document why it needs to be in package.json.

title: web-vitals listed in dependencies but never imported in src/
description: The web-vitals package is in production dependencies but is never imported in any source file. This suggests it was added speculatively but never wired up for performance monitoring.
fix: Either implement web-vitals reporting (e.g., in a reportWebVitals function or via next/third-parties) or remove the dependency.

title: livekit-server-sdk listed in dependencies but never imported in src/
description: The livekit-server-sdk package is in production dependencies but search for 'from livekit-server-sdk' returns zero results. This appears to be unused dead weight.
fix: Remove livekit-server-sdk from dependencies if unused.

title: npm audit with continue-on-error: true — security findings silently ignored
description: The dependency-audit job in the security scan workflow uses 'continue-on-error: true' for npm audit (line 42) and Snyk (line 46). This means known vulnerabilities in dependencies will never block a deployment. The workflow will always appear green even with critical CVEs.
fix: Remove continue-on-error from npm audit. Configure npm audit to fail only on critical/high severity (--audit-level=high). For Snyk, keep continue-on-error only if SNYK_TOKEN is optional, but add a separate required step for npm audit.

title: Test step in CI uses continue-on-error: true — test failures silently pass
description: In the security-scan.yml build-test job, 'npm test' runs with continue-on-error: true (line 140). Test failures will never block the CI pipeline. Combined with the fact that there are ZERO unit test files in src/__tests__/, this means the test step is entirely theatrical — it does nothing and can never fail.
fix: Once unit tests are written, remove continue-on-error: true. In the meantime, document that the test step is a placeholder.

title: Deploy pipeline does not run 'npm test' or any test suite before deploying
description: The deploy-azure.yml workflow builds the application but does NOT run any tests (unit, integration, or E2E) before deploying to production. A code change that passes the build but breaks runtime behavior will be deployed directly to production.
fix: Add a test step after the build step in the deploy job. At minimum run 'npm test'. Ideally, add a smoke test that starts the server and hits /api/health.

title: No staging environment — code deploys directly to production
description: The deploy workflow only deploys to 'production' by default. While there is a workflow_dispatch option for 'staging', no staging environment URL or configuration is defined. Every push to main goes straight to the live production site.
fix: Set up a staging Azure App Service slot. Deploy to staging first, run smoke tests, then swap slots for zero-downtime production deployment.

title: cancel-in-progress: true may break deployments mid-flight
description: The concurrency setting cancel-in-progress: true (line 50) means a new push to main will cancel an in-progress deployment. If the previous deployment has already applied Azure settings or pushed code but hasn't completed the health check, this can leave the app in an inconsistent state.
fix: Change to cancel-in-progress: false so deployments complete fully. If rapid iteration is needed, use a queue-based approach where new deploys wait for the current one to finish.

title: CodeQL init and analyze should be in separate steps with autobuild
description: The SAST job initializes CodeQL (line 85) and runs analyze (line 90) without an autobuild step between them. For JavaScript/TypeScript, CodeQL typically needs the code to be built to perform accurate analysis. The init step should be followed by 'actions/autobuild' before analyze.
fix: Add 'uses: github/codeql-action/autobuild@v3' between the init and analyze steps.

title: Playwright E2E tests only run weekly — not on PRs or pushes
description: The Playwright workflow runs on a weekly schedule (Monday 6 AM) and manual dispatch only. It does not run on pull requests or pushes. This means E2E regressions can be introduced and not caught for up to a week.
fix: Add 'pull_request: branches: [main]' trigger to run E2E tests on PRs. Use a matrix with a subset of tests for PR runs and the full suite for the weekly run.

title: Playwright tests run without starting the server in CI
description: The CI workflow builds the application but does not start the Next.js server before running 'npx playwright test'. The playwright.config.ts only starts the webServer when NOT in CI (process.env.CI ? undefined : { ... }). Without a running server, all E2E tests will fail with connection refused errors.
fix: Add a step before Playwright tests: 'npm run start &' with a wait-on script, or configure webServer in playwright.config.ts to also run in CI using 'npm run start'.

title: Notify job only logs to console — no actual notification delivery
description: The 'notify' job in deploy-azure.yml only uses 'echo' to print success/failure messages. There is no Slack webhook, email, Teams notification, or any real alerting mechanism. Deploy failures will only be visible in the GitHub Actions UI.
fix: Integrate a notification service: Slack webhook (actions/slack-notify), email via SendGrid, or Teams connector. At minimum, use GitHub's built-in notification for failed workflows.

title: Husky configured in package.json but .husky directory is missing
description: Husky v8 is listed in devDependencies and lint-staged is configured in package.json, but the .husky/ directory does not exist. This means pre-commit hooks (eslint, prettier) are not installed and not running. Code can be committed without linting or formatting.
fix: Run 'npx husky install' to create the .husky directory, then 'npx husky add .husky/pre-commit "npx lint-staged"' to set up the pre-commit hook. Add 'prepare': 'husky install' to package.json scripts.

title: No global rate limiting in middleware — all routes unthrottled
description: The middleware.ts file handles auth, CORS, and security headers but has NO rate limiting logic. While individual routes (admin API, cron, webhooks) implement their own rate limiting via rate-limiter-flexible, there is no global rate limit to prevent DDoS or brute force attacks on unauthenticated endpoints like /auth/signin, /api/auth/*, or public APIs.
fix: Add a global rate limiter in middleware.ts using the existing rate-limiter-flexible package. Apply conservative limits (e.g., 100 req/min) for auth endpoints and generous limits (e.g., 1000 req/min) for public pages. Use x-forwarded-for or x-azure-clientip for client identification.

title: CORS Access-Control-Allow-Credentials header missing — cookies not sent cross-origin
description: The middleware sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin for API routes but never sets Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. If any frontend client needs to send authentication cookies cross-origin (e.g., from a custom domain to the API), the browser will block the cookies. Additionally, using a specific origin (not *) without credentials means the CORS setup may not match all client needs.
fix: If cross-origin cookie sending is needed, add 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true' to the CORS response headers. If not needed, document this decision.

title: CSP uses unsafe-inline for both scripts and styles — XSS protection weakened
description: The Content-Security-Policy in both middleware.ts and next.config.js uses 'unsafe-inline' for script-src and style-src. The comments explain this is required by Next.js styled-jsx and Radix UI, but 'unsafe-inline' for scripts significantly weakens XSS protection. Any injected inline script will be allowed to execute.
fix: Implement nonce-based CSP. Next.js 14+ supports CSP nonces via middleware (set a random nonce per request, pass it via header, configure Next.js to use it). This would allow removing 'unsafe-inline' from script-src while keeping Next.js functionality.

title: Duplicate CSP definition in next.config.js and middleware.ts
description: The Content-Security-Policy is defined in both next.config.js (headers function, line 112-131) and middleware.ts (addSecurityHeaders function, line 37-54). These two definitions must be kept in sync manually. If one is updated without the other, different routes will have different security policies, creating inconsistencies.
fix: Define CSP directives in a single shared module (e.g., src/lib/csp.ts) and import it in both next.config.js and middleware.ts. This ensures a single source of truth.

title: React 18 pinned while Next.js 15 supports React 19 — missing concurrent features
description: React is pinned to ^18.2.0 while Next.js ^15.5.12 supports React 19. React 19 includes performance improvements (compiler, automatic memoization, use() hook, server components improvements) that Next.js 15 is designed to leverage.
fix: Upgrade to React 19 and react-dom 19. Test thoroughly as some third-party component libraries may need updates.

title: ESLint 8 while eslint-config-next 15 is installed — version mismatch risk
description: ESLint is pinned to ^8.56.0 but eslint-config-next is ^15.1.0. While this works, ESLint 9 has been stable since April 2024 and eslint-config-next 15 is optimized for it. The @typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin is also at v6 when v8 is current.
fix: Upgrade to ESLint 9, @typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin v8, and update eslint config to flat config format if desired.

title: .env.example missing 20+ env vars used in production code
description: Multiple environment variables referenced in the codebase are not documented in .env.example: SENTRY_DSN, ZAPIER_API_KEY, ZAPIER_WEBHOOK_SECRET, SHIPPING_WEBHOOK_SECRET, TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN, META_APP_SECRET, TEAMS_WEBHOOK_SECRET, WEBEX_WEBHOOK_SECRET, STRIPE_ATTITUDES_WEBHOOK_SECRET, VOIP_CDR_WEBHOOK_SECRET (duplicate listing without value), EMAIL_WEBHOOK_SECRET, RESEND_WEBHOOK_SECRET, SITE_URL, AUTH_SECRET, WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_URL, FACEBOOK_APP_SECRET, and others. A developer setting up the project will not know these are needed until runtime errors occur.
fix: Audit all process.env.* references in src/ and add every variable to .env.example with a clear description and example value. Group by feature area.

title: GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID defined twice in .env.example
description: GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID and GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET appear twice in .env.example: once under 'OAUTH PROVIDERS' (lines 18-19) and again under 'PLATFORM INTEGRATIONS' (lines 96-97). This is confusing and could lead to different values being set for what should be the same credential.
fix: Remove the duplicate entries. Add a comment in the Platform Integrations section referencing the OAuth section: '# Google (Meet + YouTube): uses GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID and GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET above'.

title: .gitignore missing entries for common sensitive and generated files
description: The .gitignore is missing entries for: .env.staging, .env.preview (deployment environments), *.sqlite (local DB testing), playwright-report/ (generated test reports), e2e/results/ (test artifacts), .turbo/ (Turbopack cache), and AUDIT_CYCLES/ (audit output). The prisma/migrations/ exclusion (line 43) may also be problematic — migrations should typically be committed for production deployments.
fix: Add missing entries. Review whether prisma/migrations/ should be committed (it should, for deterministic production deployments via 'prisma migrate deploy').

title: No 'prepare' script for husky — hooks not auto-installed after npm install
description: Husky v8 requires a 'prepare' script in package.json to automatically install Git hooks after 'npm install'. Without it, developers who clone and install will not have pre-commit hooks active.
fix: Add '"prepare": "husky install"' to the scripts section of package.json.

title: No 'test:e2e' script — E2E tests not easily runnable
description: There is no 'test:e2e' script in package.json to run Playwright tests. Developers must know to run 'npx playwright test' manually. The 'test' script only runs Jest (which has no test files).
fix: Add '"test:e2e": "playwright test"' and '"test:all": "npm run test && npm run test:e2e"' to scripts.

title: No 'test:coverage' script for coverage reporting
description: While jest.config.js has collectCoverageFrom configured, there is no script to easily run tests with coverage. There is also no coverage threshold configuration.
fix: Add '"test:coverage": "jest --coverage --coverageThreshold='{"global":{"branches":50,"functions":50,"lines":50,"statements":50}}'"' to scripts.

title: next.config.js uses require() — should be next.config.mjs for ESM consistency
description: next.config.js uses CommonJS require() while the project targets ES modules (module: esnext in tsconfig). Next.js 15 supports next.config.mjs natively. Using ESM would be more consistent with the rest of the codebase.
fix: Rename to next.config.mjs and convert require() to import. This is a low-priority cosmetic improvement.

title: Database backup has no retention policy or rotation
description: The backup-database.yml workflow runs twice daily but has no configuration for backup rotation or retention. Old backups will accumulate indefinitely in Azure Blob Storage, increasing storage costs. There is also no verification step to confirm the backup is valid (e.g., restoring to a test DB).
fix: Add a lifecycle policy to the Azure Blob Storage container to automatically delete backups older than 30 days. Add a weekly backup verification job that restores the latest backup to a temp database and runs a basic query.

title: Backup failure notification is echo-only — no real alerting
description: When the database backup fails, the workflow only outputs a GitHub Actions error annotation (echo '::error::'). There is no email, Slack, or PagerDuty notification. A failed backup could go unnoticed for days until a disaster requires restoration.
fix: Add a proper notification mechanism (Slack webhook, email) for backup failures. This is critical for a production database.

title: TypeScript strict mode enabled but missing noUncheckedIndexedAccess
description: While tsconfig.json has strict: true and good additional checks (noImplicitAny, noImplicitReturns, noUnusedLocals, noUnusedParameters), it is missing noUncheckedIndexedAccess. This means array[index] returns T instead of T | undefined, hiding potential runtime errors from out-of-bounds access.
fix: Add '"noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true' to compilerOptions. This will require fixing some code that assumes array access always returns a value.

title: TypeScript target ES2022 could be updated to ES2023 for Object.groupBy and other features
description: The TypeScript target is ES2022. Node.js 20 (required by the engines field) supports ES2023 features including Object.groupBy, Array.findLast, and other improvements.
fix: Update target to ES2023 to leverage newer JavaScript features natively.

title: Platform webhook skips signature verification when secret not configured
description: The Stripe Attitudes (Koraline SaaS) webhook at /api/platform/webhook returns { received: true } with 200 OK when STRIPE_ATTITUDES_WEBHOOK_SECRET is not configured or contains 'to_configure'. This means ANY request to this endpoint will be accepted and acknowledged without verification, potentially allowing forged webhook events to modify tenant subscriptions and billing status.
fix: Return 503 (Service Unavailable) when the webhook secret is not configured, similar to how other webhook endpoints handle this case. Never return 200 for unverified webhooks.

title: 34 cron jobs with no centralized scheduler or monitoring dashboard
description: There are 34 cron job endpoints under /api/cron/. Each uses CRON_SECRET for authentication and withJobLock for overlap prevention, which is good. However, there is no centralized scheduler configuration (like a crontab or Vercel cron config) and no monitoring dashboard to track execution history, success/failure rates, and latency. The admin has /admin/cron-stats but no automated alerting for cron failures.
fix: Create a centralized cron configuration file documenting all 34 jobs with their schedules. Implement cron execution logging to a database table (CronExecution: jobName, startedAt, finishedAt, status, error). Add an alert when a cron job fails 3 times consecutively.

title: Winston logger writes to filesystem — fails on Azure read-only deployments
description: The logger imports 'fs' and 'path' and likely writes log files to the filesystem. Per KB-PP-BUILD-006, Azure with WEBSITE_RUN_FROM_PACKAGE=1 mounts the deployment as read-only. File-based logging will fail silently or crash. Production logs should go to stdout/stderr for Azure App Service to capture.
fix: Ensure the production transport is Console only (stdout/stderr). Remove or conditionally disable any File transports when WEBSITE_RUN_FROM_PACKAGE=1 or NODE_ENV=production. Azure App Service captures stdout automatically.

title: Health check reports Sentry as 'not configured' — no APM in production
description: The health check at /api/health shows Sentry is not configured (SENTRY_DSN missing). While there are instrumentation files (src/instrumentation.ts, src/lib/apm.ts), the actual Sentry DSN is not set. This means there is no application performance monitoring, error tracking, or exception alerting in production.
fix: Set up a Sentry project and configure SENTRY_DSN in Azure App Service settings. Install @sentry/nextjs and configure the Sentry SDK properly for Next.js 15 App Router.

title: Health check exposes database error messages — potential information disclosure
description: When the database check fails, the error message is included in the response: 'Database connection failed: {errorMsg}'. This could expose connection strings, host names, or internal error details to anyone calling /api/health.
fix: In production, replace the detailed error message with a generic 'Database connection failed' without the specific error. Log the detailed error server-side only.

title: isomorphic-dompurify at release candidate (rc.2) in production dependencies
description: isomorphic-dompurify@3.0.0-rc.2 is a release candidate, not a stable release. Release candidates may have unresolved bugs. For a sanitization library that protects against XSS, stability is critical.
fix: Pin to the latest stable version of isomorphic-dompurify, or use dompurify (also in deps) directly with proper server/client handling.

title: Both dompurify and isomorphic-dompurify in dependencies — redundant packages
description: Both dompurify and isomorphic-dompurify are listed in dependencies. isomorphic-dompurify is a wrapper around dompurify that works in both Node.js and browser environments. Having both is redundant and confusing.
fix: Use only isomorphic-dompurify and remove the direct dompurify dependency (it will be included as a transitive dependency).

title: Missing date-fns from optimizePackageImports may cause larger bundles
description: date-fns is listed in experimental.optimizePackageImports but is NOT in package.json dependencies. This import optimization has no effect. Meanwhile, packages like exceljs, bullmq, stripe, and openai that ARE used could benefit from tree-shaking optimization.
fix: Remove date-fns from optimizePackageImports since it is not a dependency. Add other heavy packages that would benefit from tree-shaking.

title: Missing @radix-ui/react-icons from optimizePackageImports — using non-existent package
description: @radix-ui/react-icons is listed in optimizePackageImports but is not in package.json. The project uses lucide-react for icons instead. This optimization directive has no effect.
fix: Remove @radix-ui/react-icons from optimizePackageImports. Add packages that are actually used and could benefit: lucide-react is already there.

title: prisma devDependency version (^5.8.0) may drift from @prisma/client (^5.8.0)
description: Both prisma (CLI, devDep) and @prisma/client (runtime, dep) are at ^5.8.0. The ^ allows minor version upgrades independently, meaning one could be 5.8.x while the other is 5.22.x. Prisma requires client and CLI versions to match exactly.
fix: Pin both prisma and @prisma/client to the exact same version (e.g., "5.22.0" without ^). Update them together.

title: Prisma version in CI (5.22.0) may differ from package.json (^5.8.0)
description: The deploy workflow installs prisma@5.22.0 explicitly for schema sync (line 388), while package.json has ^5.8.0. This version mismatch could cause schema push to behave differently in CI vs local development.
fix: Align the Prisma version in the deploy workflow with the version in package.json. Better yet, use the already-installed version from npm ci instead of reinstalling.

title: No Dockerfile — manual Azure deployment only, no containerization option
description: There is no Dockerfile or docker-compose.yml in the project. The deployment relies entirely on Azure App Service with standalone build. This limits deployment options and makes local production testing difficult. The security-scan.yml even has a disabled container-scan job (if: false) waiting for a Dockerfile.
fix: Create a Dockerfile for the standalone Next.js build. This enables: local production testing, alternative hosting (Railway, Fly.io, AWS ECS), faster Azure deployments via container registry, and enables the container security scan in CI.

title: Semgrep uses deprecated action (returntocorp/semgrep-action@v1)
description: The SAST scan uses returntocorp/semgrep-action@v1 which is maintained by Semgrep but may be outdated. Semgrep has moved to semgrep/semgrep-action for newer releases.
fix: Update to 'uses: semgrep/semgrep-action@v1' or the latest version from the Semgrep organization.

title: ZERO unit test files exist — 0% unit test coverage
description: The src/__tests__/ directory does not exist and contains no test files. Jest is configured (jest.config.js) with test patterns for src/__tests__/**/*.test.ts but there are literally zero test files. The 'npm test' command will pass vacuously with 0 tests run. For a production e-commerce platform handling payments, authentication, and accounting, this is a critical gap. Key untested areas: Stripe payment processing, order state machine, accounting journal entries, authentication flow, CSRF protection, rate limiting, email sending, inventory management.
fix: Prioritize unit tests for: (1) Payment webhook processing (src/app/api/payments/webhook), (2) Order state machine (src/lib/order-status-machine), (3) Authentication helpers (src/lib/auth), (4) Accounting calculations (src/lib/accounting), (5) Rate limiting (src/lib/rate-limiter), (6) Email templates (src/lib/email). Target 60% coverage for critical paths.

title: Jest setup references jest.polyfills.js and jest.setup.js that may not exist
description: jest.config.js references '<rootDir>/jest.polyfills.js' and '<rootDir>/jest.setup.js' as setup files. If these files don't exist, Jest will crash when trying to run. Combined with zero test files, this means the test infrastructure has never been validated.
fix: Create jest.polyfills.js (e.g., with TextEncoder/TextDecoder polyfills for jsdom) and jest.setup.js (e.g., with @testing-library/jest-dom imports). Verify by running 'npm test'.

title: E2E tests are minimal stubs — only 378 total lines across 14 files
description: The 14 E2E test files total only 378 lines (~27 lines each). For a platform with 223+ admin pages, this coverage is negligible. Most spec files are likely just testing that pages load without crashes, not testing actual functionality (form submissions, payment flows, CRUD operations).
fix: Expand E2E tests to cover critical user journeys: (1) Product purchase flow, (2) Admin order management, (3) User registration and login, (4) Cart and checkout, (5) Admin CRUD for products/categories. Target at least 50 meaningful test cases.

title: Playwright webServer not started in CI — tests likely fail
description: The Playwright config sets webServer to undefined when process.env.CI is true (line 40). The CI workflow (playwright.yml) builds the app but never starts it before running tests. This means all E2E tests in CI will fail with ECONNREFUSED as no server is listening on localhost:3000.
fix: In playwright.yml, add a step to start the production build: 'npx next start &' followed by 'npx wait-on http://localhost:3000'. Alternatively, configure the webServer in playwright.config.ts for CI: command: 'npm run start', reuseExistingServer: false.

title: Testing libraries installed but unused — @testing-library/jest-dom and @testing-library/react
description: @testing-library/jest-dom and @testing-library/react are in devDependencies but with zero test files in src/__tests__/, they serve no purpose. This is not inherently harmful but indicates testing infrastructure was set up but never used.
fix: Either write tests using these libraries or remove them to reduce install time. Writing tests is strongly preferred.

title: @types/jest in devDependencies but jest-environment-jsdom version mismatch
description: @types/jest is at ^30.0.0 while jest itself is at ^29.7.0 and jest-environment-jsdom is at ^30.2.0. Version mismatches between Jest packages can cause subtle test failures or type errors.
fix: Align all Jest-related packages to the same major version. Either upgrade jest to v30 or downgrade @types/jest and jest-environment-jsdom to v29.

title: No integration test suite for API routes
description: There are no integration tests for the 200+ API routes. API routes handle critical business logic (payments, orders, accounting, CRM) and could have subtle bugs that unit tests miss but integration tests would catch (e.g., database constraints, auth middleware behavior, response shapes).
fix: Create an integration test suite using supertest or Next.js's test utilities. Priority routes: /api/payments/webhook, /api/admin/orders, /api/auth/*, /api/cron/*.

title: Order state machine has no unit tests — critical business logic untested
description: The order status machine (validateTransition) is used by multiple webhook handlers and API routes but has zero unit tests. An invalid state transition could allow orders to go from DELIVERED back to PENDING, or skip the SHIPPED state.
fix: Write exhaustive unit tests for validateTransition() covering all valid transitions, invalid transitions, and edge cases (terminal states, self-transitions).

title: Financial calculation library has no unit tests — rounding errors possible
description: The decimal-calculator library (using decimal.js) handles money arithmetic for the entire platform. Functions like multiply, add, subtract, applyRate are used in checkout, accounting, and tax calculations. Zero unit tests means rounding errors or precision bugs could cause financial discrepancies.
fix: Write comprehensive unit tests for all decimal-calculator functions with edge cases: very large numbers, very small fractions, rounding modes, currency-specific precision.

title: Cron lock mechanism has no tests — concurrent execution prevention untested
description: The withJobLock function prevents concurrent cron execution using Redis SETNX with in-memory fallback. This critical infrastructure component has no tests to verify lock acquisition, release, timeout handling, and fallback behavior.
fix: Write unit tests mocking Redis to verify: lock acquisition, lock release on completion, lock release on error, timeout via AbortController, fallback to in-memory when Redis is unavailable.

title: Brute force protection has no tests — security mechanism unverified
description: The brute force protection module is used in authentication flows but has no unit tests. If the protection fails silently, attackers could attempt unlimited login attempts without being blocked.
fix: Write tests verifying: lockout after N failed attempts, lockout duration, reset after successful login, IP-based vs account-based lockout, Redis fallback behavior.

title: Security headers E2E test is only 43 lines — likely incomplete
description: The security headers test file is only 43 lines. Given the extensive security header configuration in middleware.ts and next.config.js (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, Permissions-Policy, etc.), this test likely checks only a subset of headers.
fix: Expand the security headers test to verify ALL security headers on multiple route types: public pages, API routes, admin pages, static files, and uploads.

title: Core Web Vitals test exists but web-vitals package is unused in application code
description: There is a performance test for Core Web Vitals (35 lines) but the web-vitals npm package is never imported in the application code. This test likely uses Playwright performance APIs rather than the web-vitals library, making the npm dependency even more clearly unused.
fix: Either integrate web-vitals into the application (e.g., via reportWebVitals) or remove the npm dependency. Keep the Playwright-based performance test.

title: No BullMQ queue health check in /api/health
description: The application uses BullMQ for background job processing (4 files in src/lib reference bullmq), but the health check endpoint does not verify queue connectivity or report queue metrics (pending jobs, failed jobs, delayed jobs). A stalled queue would not be detected by the health check.
fix: Add a BullMQ health check in /api/health that verifies Redis connection (for queue backend) and reports queue status (active, waiting, failed counts).

title: No disk space check in health endpoint — Azure storage could fill
description: The health check monitors memory but not disk space. On Azure App Service, the /home directory has limited storage. If uploads, logs, or temp files fill the disk, the application will crash without the health check detecting it.
fix: Add a disk space check using fs.statfs('/home') or equivalent. Warn at 80% usage, fail at 95%.

title: Request logging uses Math.random() for sampling — not cryptographically random
description: The middleware uses Math.random() < 0.1 for log sampling (line 247). While not a security vulnerability (it's just for sampling rate), Math.random() is predictable. More importantly, the sampling rate (10%) is hardcoded with no configuration option.
fix: Make the sampling rate configurable via an environment variable (e.g., LOG_SAMPLING_RATE=0.1). The use of Math.random() for sampling is acceptable but consider using crypto.getRandomValues for consistency with the rest of the security-conscious codebase.

title: No request duration tracking in middleware — no latency observability
description: The middleware generates a request start time (line 251) but never calculates or reports request duration. There is no way to identify slow requests or measure P50/P95/P99 latency without APM (Sentry is not configured).
fix: Set x-request-start header with Date.now() for downstream duration calculation, or implement duration tracking via a response interceptor. Better yet, configure Sentry APM for automatic request duration tracking.

title: No bundle size monitoring or limits configured
description: @next/bundle-analyzer is in devDependencies and configured in next.config.js, but there is no automated bundle size check in CI. JavaScript bundle sizes could grow unchecked, degrading page load performance.
fix: Add a CI step that runs the build with ANALYZE=true and compares bundle sizes against a baseline. Use @next/bundle-analyzer or bundlewatch to set size limits.

title: Cron jobs have no timeout — can run indefinitely consuming resources
description: While the cron-lock module has a DEFAULT_MAX_DURATION_MS of 5 minutes with AbortController timeout, individual cron jobs can override this with arbitrarily long timeouts or disable timeout protection entirely. If a cron job enters an infinite loop or deadlock, it will consume server resources indefinitely.
fix: Enforce a hard maximum timeout (e.g., 10 minutes) in the withJobLock wrapper that cannot be overridden. Log and alert when jobs approach the timeout limit.

title: REDIS_URL is optional but critical for rate limiting, cron locks, and idempotency
description: REDIS_URL is listed as optional in .env.example, but the application relies on Redis for: rate limiting (21 files), cron job locks (distributed), webhook idempotency (all webhook endpoints), and session caching. Without Redis, all these features degrade to in-memory fallbacks that don't work across multiple instances and lose state on restart.
fix: Document Redis as a required dependency for production deployments. Add a health check warning when Redis is unavailable in production. Consider failing fast in production if Redis is not configured.

title: No dependency update automation (Dependabot/Renovate) configured
description: There is no .github/dependabot.yml or renovate.json configuration. Dependencies will only be updated manually. Given the large dependency tree (70+ packages), security patches and bug fixes could be missed for weeks or months.
fix: Add .github/dependabot.yml with weekly npm updates, or configure Renovate for more fine-grained control. Group minor/patch updates into single PRs.

title: Build step does not set all env vars — NEXT_PUBLIC vars may embed wrong values
description: The build step only sets NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_NAME and NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL (lines 92-93), but .env.example defines NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_DESCRIPTION, NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPPORT_EMAIL, NEXT_PUBLIC_CONTACT_EMAIL, and others. Since NEXT_PUBLIC_ vars are inlined at build time, missing values during the build will result in empty strings or undefined in the client bundle.
fix: Add all NEXT_PUBLIC_* environment variables to the build step, either from GitHub Secrets or as static values.

title: Unknown domain defaults to 'biocycle' tenant — should return error for unknown hosts
description: The resolveTenantFromHost function defaults any unknown hostname to { tenantSlug: 'biocycle', isSuperAdmin: false } (line 184). This means any random domain pointing to the server will serve the BioCycle tenant content. In a multi-tenant SaaS, unknown domains should return a 404 or redirect to the main site.
fix: For unknown hostnames, return a dedicated error page or redirect to attitudes.vip. Log the unknown hostname for monitoring purposes.

title: Build artifact retention is only 1 day — cannot rollback after 24 hours
description: The standalone-package artifact has retention-days: 1 (line 178). If a production issue is discovered after 24 hours, the previous build artifact is gone and cannot be redeployed without rebuilding from the previous commit.
fix: Increase retention to 7 days for production artifacts. This allows quick rollbacks within a week.

title: Image device sizes include 2048px but no 3840px for 4K displays
description: The image deviceSizes list goes up to 2048px. With increasing 4K (3840px) display adoption, the next/image component may serve upscaled 2048px images to 4K users, resulting in blurry product images.
fix: Add 3840 to deviceSizes for 4K display support: deviceSizes: [640, 750, 828, 1080, 1200, 1920, 2048, 3840].

title: No package-lock.json integrity verification in CI — supply chain risk
description: While npm ci is used (which verifies against package-lock.json), there is no explicit integrity check or checksum verification step in the CI pipeline. A compromised npm registry could serve malicious packages.
fix: Consider using npm audit signatures (npm audit signatures --registry https://registry.npmjs.org) in CI to verify package provenance.

title: In-memory rate limiting on webhooks does not work across multiple instances
description: Both shipping webhook and email-bounce webhook use in-memory rate limiting (Map/object in process memory). The email-bounce route even documents this limitation (line 20-21): 'This state lives in the Node.js process memory and is NOT distributed.' If Azure scales to multiple instances, each instance has its own counter, effectively multiplying the rate limit.
fix: Migrate webhook rate limiting to Redis-backed rate limiting using the existing rate-limiter-flexible package. This ensures rate limits work correctly across multiple instances.

title: In-memory event deduplication cache has no LRU eviction timer
description: The Stripe webhook uses a Map-based LRU cache for event deduplication (DEDUP_CACHE_MAX=5000). However, entries are only evicted when the cache is full (line 74-76), not when they expire (DEDUP_TTL_MS=10min). Stale entries persist until pushed out by new entries, potentially consuming unnecessary memory.
fix: Implement a periodic cleanup (setInterval every 5 minutes) to remove entries older than DEDUP_TTL_MS, or use a proper LRU cache library like lru-cache.

title: secureCookie: false for auth session — cookies sent over HTTP
description: The getToken call uses secureCookie: false (line 358). The comment explains this is because Azure TLS terminates at the load balancer, so the cookie doesn't have the __Secure- prefix. However, this also means the session cookie can be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections during development, making it vulnerable to session hijacking on non-HTTPS connections.
fix: Set secureCookie to process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production' or to true when HSTS is enforced. The cookie name 'authjs.session-token' should still work with Azure's TLS termination. Alternatively, ensure HSTS (already configured) prevents any HTTP access.

title: No pre-push or pre-commit build verification hook
description: While lint-staged is configured for eslint and prettier on commit, there is no pre-push hook to verify that 'npm run build' succeeds before pushing. Per the deploy-azure-zero-failure rule, builds must pass locally before pushing, but this is not enforced automatically.
fix: Add a pre-push hook via husky that runs 'npx prisma generate && npm run build' to prevent pushing broken code.

title: serverExternalPackages includes jsdom but jsdom is not a direct dependency
description: serverExternalPackages lists jsdom as an external package, but jsdom is not in package.json dependencies. It is a transitive dependency of isomorphic-dompurify. While this works, it is fragile — if isomorphic-dompurify changes its internal implementation, the exclusion may become unnecessary or insufficient.
fix: Add a comment explaining the transitive dependency chain: isomorphic-dompurify -> jsdom. Consider monitoring for changes in isomorphic-dompurify's implementation.

title: prisma/migrations/ is gitignored — production migrations not tracked
description: The .gitignore excludes prisma/migrations/. For development using 'prisma db push', this is fine. But for production, migrations should be tracked in version control for deterministic schema changes via 'prisma migrate deploy'. Without tracked migrations, there is no audit trail of schema changes.
fix: Remove prisma/migrations/ from .gitignore when moving to migration-based schema management. For now, document that 'prisma db push' is used for both dev and production.

title: @types/jsonwebtoken in devDependencies but jsonwebtoken not in dependencies
description: @types/jsonwebtoken is listed in devDependencies but jsonwebtoken is not in dependencies. The project uses jose (a modern JWT library) instead. The @types/jsonwebtoken package is unnecessary.
fix: Remove @types/jsonwebtoken from devDependencies since jsonwebtoken is not used.

title: WhatsApp webhook swallows errors and returns 200 — silent failures
description: The WhatsApp webhook catch block (line 135-141) logs the error but returns { success: true } with 200 status. The comment says this prevents Twilio retries, but it also means legitimate processing errors are silently swallowed. The webhook will appear successful even when message processing completely fails.
fix: Track error counts and alert when error rate exceeds a threshold. Consider returning 500 for non-transient errors and 200 only for duplicate/already-processed messages. Twilio retry behavior can be controlled via the webhook configuration.

title: Health endpoint has no authentication — anyone can see infrastructure status
description: The /api/health endpoint is publicly accessible with no authentication. The readiness check reveals: database connectivity status, Redis status, payment provider configuration, email provider configuration, memory usage, Sentry configuration, and auth configuration. While individual details are sanitized, the aggregate information gives attackers a detailed picture of the infrastructure.
fix: Require authentication for the full readiness check (?type=readiness). Keep the liveness check (?type=live) and HEAD request public for load balancers. Alternatively, add an API key requirement for the detailed health check.

title: 90+ scripts in scripts/ directory — no documentation or cleanup
description: The scripts/ directory contains 90+ files including many one-off migration scripts, translation tools, and debugging utilities (e.g., find-keys-batch4.js, find-keys-batch5.js, list-remaining.js, dump-remaining-by-ns.js). Many of these are likely obsolete but remain in the repository, increasing cognitive load.
fix: Audit the scripts/ directory. Move one-off migration scripts to a scripts/archive/ folder. Document active scripts in a scripts/README.md with usage instructions.

title: Railway vs Azure conditional output mode — fragile environment detection
description: The standalone output mode is conditionally disabled for Railway via process.env.RAILWAY_ENVIRONMENT (line 9). This means the build output format changes based on which platform is deploying. If both platforms are used simultaneously, the build artifacts will differ, making debugging harder.
fix: Document this conditional clearly. Consider using an explicit BUILD_OUTPUT_MODE env var instead of detecting the platform implicitly.

title: No rollback mechanism — failed deployments require manual intervention
description: The deploy pipeline has no automated rollback capability. If a deployment passes the health check but causes runtime issues (e.g., specific pages crash, API errors), there is no way to automatically revert to the previous version. The only option is a manual git revert + new deployment (13+ minutes).
fix: Implement Azure deployment slots: deploy to a staging slot first, run smoke tests, then swap to production. If post-swap health checks fail, auto-swap back. This gives instant rollback capability.
